title: Present perfect continuous
explanation:
Present perfect continuous

have / has been + verb-ing

We use it for actions that started in the past and continue now, or actions that have recently stopped and have a visible result.

  • I have been working here for six years.
  • She is tired because she has been studying all day.

questions.text: I [*have been waiting|have waited|am waiting] for the bus for half an hour.
questions.feedback: For half an hour suggests an activity continuing until now.

questions.text: She is wet because it [*has been raining|has rained|rained] all afternoon.
How long [*have you been learning|have you learnt|are you learning] English?
questions.feedback: We ask about duration with the present perfect continuous.

questions.text: They [*have been living|live|are living] in Dublin since January.
questions.feedback: Since January shows an unfinished period.

questions.text: He is exhausted because he [*has been running|has run|ran] for an hour.

exercise_sets.title: Exercise 2
exercise_sets.instructions: Choose the correct option for each gap.

questions.text: We ____ on this project all week. | *have been working | worked | are working
questions.feedback: All week points to an unfinished activity.

questions.text: Why are your hands dirty? I ____ in the garden. | *have been digging | dug | am digging
She ____ too much coffee recently. | *has been drinking | drank | has drunk
It ____ since early morning. | *has been snowing | snowed | is snowing
We [*have been trying|tried|are trying] to call you all day.
questions.feedback: All day suggests duration up to now.

questions.text: He [*has been teaching|taught|teaches] at this school for ten years.
My eyes hurt because I [*have been looking|looked|have looked] at the screen too long.
They [*have been travelling|travelled|are travelling] around South America since May.
questions.feedback: Since May suggests a continuing journey.

questions.text: I [*have not been sleeping|did not sleep|am not sleeping] well recently.
